summary: In this interview, Luke Kim of Spice AI explains how his startup addresses the gap in serving data lakehouse files in production. Spice AI functions as a CDN for databases, providing a caching and materialization layer that sits between applications and data warehouses/lakes. The architecture prioritizes single-node optimization with horizontal scalability, drawing from Kim's experience building Dapr at Microsoft. Spice can be deployed as sidecars alongside applications, stackable across infrastructure tiers from edge to cloud, minimizing data transit while maintaining query performance. The system supports both proactive materialization and reactive caching, with CDC support for consistency across distributed replicas.
key_points: The data ecosystem has matured for writes and transforms (ETL/ELT), but a significant gap remains for read and query scenarios in production

Spice AI inverts the traditional application-data warehouse relationship by deploying lightweight, single-node optimized runtimes alongside each application rather than having many applications query a single cluster
The 'Big Data is Dead' thesis applies here: the working set of data queried day-to-day is typically 2-3 orders of magnitude smaller than what an application could query
Sidecars provide language/runtime separation while maintaining performance through fast kernel-level local loopback, making building blocks portable and platform agnostic
Tiered storage architecture allows Spice instances to be stacked from edge to cloud, with CDC over Arrow Flight for real-time consistency
money_quotes: The history of the technology industry is a story of turning lower layers of the application stack into commoditized building blocks.
Like the cloud infrastructure example, lower level plumbing can be slow to build, expensive, hard to maintain and get right, and often offers little differentiated business value.
Don't fight physics; minimize data transit.
The working set of data queried day-to-day is normally 2-3 orders of magnitude smaller than the data the application could query.
Spice inverts the application-data warehouse relationship to be more distributed.
Separation from the language and runtime enables common functionality to be portable and platform agnostic, while maintaining performance through a very fast kernel-level local loopback.
As you move up this stack, you want to focus on delivering user value at higher and higher levels, not deal with the lower level data plumbing.
key_insight: The missing piece in the data lakehouse ecosystem is a CDN-like caching layer that brings data closer to applications, and the sidecar pattern perfected by service meshes provides the ideal deployment model for this use case.
